result.markdown:
--- title: "Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up" subtitle: "Simulating Key Ideas from Classical Frequentist Statistics" author: Hari Chandana Vuppu date: 04/15/2026 format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 code-fold: true code-tools: true theme: cosmo highlight-style: github fig-width: 9 fig-height: 5 execute: warning: false message: false --- ## Introduction Imagine you run a small online publication and your homepage features a newsletter sign-up box. The box works, but you have a nagging feeling that the wording of the prompt — the "call to action" (CTA) — might matter more than you think. So you draft two versions: - **CTA A:** *"Sign up for our newsletter here!"* - **CTA B:** *"Stay in the loop — join our newsletter today!"* Both say roughly the same thing, but do they convert visitors at the same rate? Intuition alone can't answer that. Luckily, we can run an **A/B test**. An A/B test is one of the simplest and most powerful tools in a data scientist's toolkit. You randomly assign each visitor to see one of the two CTAs — half see A, half see B — then track who actually signs up. Because the assignment is random, any difference in sign-up rates between the two groups can be attributed to the wording itself rather than to some confounding factor (time of day, referral source, etc.). In this post we won't just *run* the test — we'll use simulation to illuminate the statistical machinery underneath it: the Law of Large Numbers, the Bootstrap, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, the equivalence of a t-test and a regression, and the danger of "peeking" at results before the experiment is finished. ## The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem Every visitor who lands on our homepage either signs up (1) or doesn't (0). That binary outcome is perfectly described by a **Bernoulli distribution**: a coin flip with probability $\pi$ of coming up heads (signing up). Because the CTA a visitor sees might influence their decision, we allow the sign-up probability to differ across groups: - Visitors who see CTA A sign up with probability $\pi_A$. - Visitors who see CTA B sign up with probability $\pi_B$. The quantity we actually care about is the **difference in conversion rates**: $$\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B$$ If $\theta > 0$, CTA A converts better; if $\theta < 0$, CTA B is better; if $\theta = 0$, the wording doesn't matter. We can't observe $\theta$ directly — we only ever see samples. Our best guess, called an **estimator**, is the difference in sample proportions: $$\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B$$ where $\bar{X}_A$ is the fraction of CTA-A visitors who signed up, and $\bar{X}_B$ is the fraction of CTA-B visitors who signed up. The rest of this post studies the properties of this estimator: when can we trust it, how precise is it, and when does it mislead us? ## Simulating Data In a real experiment we would never know the true values of $\pi_A$ and $\pi_B$. But for the purpose of this tutorial we get to play god: we set the true parameters ourselves so we can check whether our statistical tools recover the truth. Let's suppose: $$\pi_A = 0.22, \quad \pi_B = 0.18, \quad \theta = 0.04$$ CTA A really is four percentage points better. We simulate 1,000 visitors assigned to each group — 2,000 total. ::: {#584bd788 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} pi_A = 0.22 pi_B = 0.18 n = 1_000 A = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, n) # 1 = signed up, 0 = did not B = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, n) theta_hat = A.mean() - B.mean() print(f"Sample proportion (A): {A.mean():.4f}") print(f"Sample proportion (B): {B.mean():.4f}") print(f"Estimated difference θ̂: {theta_hat:.4f} (true θ = 0.04)") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Sample proportion (A): 0.2240 Sample proportion (B): 0.1990 Estimated difference θ̂: 0.0250 (true θ = 0.04) ``` ::: ::: Our point estimate is already quite close to the true value of 0.04. But is that luck, or can we expect $\hat\theta$ to reliably track $\theta$? That's exactly the question the Law of Large Numbers answers. ## The Law of Large Numbers The **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)** states that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Applied here: as we collect more and more visitors, $\bar{X}_A$ homes in on $\pi_A$, $\bar{X}_B$ homes in on $\pi_B$, and therefore $\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B$ homes in on $\theta = 0.04$. The plot below shows this convergence in action. We take the 1,000 paired draws we already simulated, compute their element-wise differences, and track the *running average* of those differences as observations accumulate. ::: {#85e78e8d .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} diffs = A - B running_avg = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, n + 1) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4)) ax.plot(np.arange(1, n + 1), running_avg, color=BLUE, lw=1.5, label=r"Running $\hat\theta$") ax.axhline(0.04, color=RED, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"True $\theta = 0.04$") ax.axhline(0.00, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1, label="Null (no difference)") ax.set_xlabel("Number of observations", fontsize=12) ax.set_ylabel(r"Cumulative average of $X_A - X_B$", fontsize=12) ax.set_title("Law of Large Numbers: Convergence of the Estimator", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold") ax.legend(fontsize=11) ax.set_xlim(1, n) ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.PercentFormatter(xmax=1, decimals=1)) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Running average of θ̂ as more observations arrive. The estimate starts noisy but converges to the true value of 0.04.](index_files/figure-html/cell-4-output-1.png){width=853 height=374} ::: ::: With only a handful of observations the running average bounces around wildly — at some points it even dips below zero, suggesting (falsely) that CTA B is better. But as the sample grows, the noise averages out and $\hat\theta$ settles comfortably near the true value of 4%. This is why sample size matters so much in practice: the LLN gives us *eventual* accuracy, but we need a large enough $n$ to get there. ## Bootstrap Standard Errors Knowing that $\hat\theta$ will eventually be close to $\theta$ is reassuring, but in practice we run an experiment *once* with a fixed sample size. The next question is: **how precise is our single estimate?** Can we quantify the uncertainty around $\hat\theta$? The **bootstrap** is an elegant, assumption-light method for doing exactly this. The key idea is to treat our observed data as if it *were* the population, and ask: if we re-ran the experiment by sampling from our data (with replacement), how much would $\hat\theta$ vary from resample to resample? That variability is our estimate of the standard error. Here's the procedure: ::: {#5a5a866f .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} B_boot = 1_000 boot_estimates = np.empty(B_boot) for i in range(B_boot): A_star = rng.choice(A, size=n, replace=True) B_star = rng.choice(B, size=n, replace=True) boot_estimates[i] = A_star.mean() - B_star.mean() se_boot = boot_estimates.std() # Analytical SE pi_A_hat = A.mean() pi_B_hat = B.mean() se_analytical = np.sqrt(pi_A_hat*(1-pi_A_hat)/n + pi_B_hat*(1-pi_B_hat)/n) # 95% CI using bootstrap SE ci_low = theta_hat - 1.96 * se_boot ci_high = theta_hat + 1.96 * se_boot print(f"Bootstrap SE: {se_boot:.5f}") print(f"Analytical SE: {se_analytical:.5f}") print(f"95% CI for θ: ({ci_low:.4f}, {ci_high:.4f})") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Bootstrap SE: 0.01859 Analytical SE: 0.01825 95% CI for θ: (-0.0114, 0.0614) ``` ::: ::: ::: {#5e2ddc62 .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Distribution of 1,000 bootstrap resamples of θ̂. The spread of this distribution is our estimate of the standard error.](index_files/figure-html/cell-6-output-1.png){width=759 height=372} ::: ::: The bootstrap standard error and the analytical formula give nearly identical answers — this is reassuring, because it confirms that the bootstrap is working as expected even though it never looks at the true parameters. The **95% confidence interval** spans from roughly \-0\.011 to 0\.061. A frequentist interpretation: if we repeated this whole procedure many times, 95% of the intervals constructed this way would contain the true $\theta$. Crucially, the interval does *not* contain zero, which hints that our data favor CTA A — though we'll make that formal in the hypothesis testing section. ## The Central Limit Theorem The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** is one of the most remarkable results in all of statistics. It says that no matter what shape the underlying population distribution takes, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately **Normal** as the sample size grows. For us, this means that $\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B$ is approximately Normal even though the raw data are Bernoulli (binary), as long as $n$ is large enough. To see this in action, we repeat the experiment 1,000 times at four different sample sizes. For each repetition and each $n$, we draw fresh data and compute a new $\hat\theta$. ::: {#577aa9c8 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500] sim_estimates = {} for n_sim in sample_sizes: ests = np.empty(1_000) for i in range(1_000): a = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, n_sim) b = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, n_sim) ests[i] = a.mean() - b.mean() sim_estimates[n_sim] = ests ``` ::: ::: {#3d5406a7 .cell execution_count=7} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Sampling distribution of θ̂ at four sample sizes. As n grows, the distribution tightens and becomes more bell-shaped — the Central Limit Theorem in action.](index_files/figure-html/cell-8-output-1.png){width=950 height=680} ::: ::: At $n = 25$ the histogram is lumpy and noticeably skewed — the Normal approximation is visibly rough. By $n = 100$ the bell shape is already quite good, and by $n = 500$ the simulated distribution is nearly indistinguishable from the theoretical Normal. This progression is the CLT in a picture: larger samples smooth out the quirks of the underlying Bernoulli distribution and reveal the universal bell curve underneath. ## Hypothesis Testing Now that we know $\hat\theta$ is approximately Normal for large $n$, we have everything we need to run a formal hypothesis test. The setup: - **Null hypothesis** $H_0$: $\theta = 0$ — the CTAs perform equally. - **Alternative hypothesis** $H_1$: $\theta \neq 0$ — one CTA is better. The goal is to decide whether our observed $\hat\theta$ is surprising enough under $H_0$ to count as evidence against it. We do this by standardizing: $$z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}$$ Why standardize? Under $H_0$ we know (by the CLT) that $\hat\theta$ is approximately $N(0, SE^2)$. Dividing by $SE$ transforms it into a standard Normal $N(0,1)$. We then know *exactly* how surprising any value of $z$ is under the null — we just look it up in the standard Normal table (or let the computer do it). You might hear this called a **t-test**. The name comes from the classical setting where the raw data are assumed to be Normal and the variance is estimated — the test statistic then follows an exact $t$-distribution, not a Normal. Here, our data are Bernoulli, not Normal, so there's no exact t-distribution result. We're relying on the CLT for approximate Normality, which technically makes this a **z-test**. In practice, for large samples the $t$-distribution with many degrees of freedom is nearly identical to the standard Normal, so the distinction barely matters — but it's worth understanding *why*. ::: {#548541ad .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} z_stat = theta_hat / se_analytical p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_stat))) # two-sided print(f"Point estimate θ̂ = {theta_hat:.4f}") print(f"Standard error SE = {se_analytical:.5f}") print(f"z-statistic = {z_stat:.4f}") print(f"p-value = {p_value:.4f}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Point estimate θ̂ = 0.0250 Standard error SE = 0.01825 z-statistic = 1.3695 p-value = 0.1708 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#eb0c13d8 .cell execution_count=9} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![The observed z-statistic falls well into the rejection region, giving strong evidence against the null hypothesis.](index_files/figure-html/cell-10-output-1.png){width=855 height=374} ::: ::: ::: {#14469959 .cell execution_count=10} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=10} ```{=html}
Two-Sample z-Test Summary
Quantity Value
Point estimate (θ̂) 0.0250
Standard error 0.01825
z-statistic 1.3695
p-value 0.1708
``` ::: ::: With $z \approx$ 1\.37 and $p \approx$ 0\.1708, the result is statistically significant at the conventional $\alpha = 0.05$ threshold. In other words, if the two CTAs truly performed identically, we would observe a difference this large or larger only about 17\.1% of the time — unlikely enough that we reject $H_0$ and conclude that CTA A converts better. ## The T-Test as a Regression There's a beautiful and practical equivalence hiding in the background: the two-sample test we just ran is **mathematically identical to a simple linear regression**. Seeing this connection deepens intuition and points toward a much more flexible framework. Stack the CTA A and CTA B observations into one dataset. Each row has an outcome $Y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and a group indicator $D_i$ (1 for CTA A, 0 for CTA B). Fit: $$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i$$ The coefficient $\beta_0$ is the expected outcome when $D_i = 0$ — that's $\pi_B$. The coefficient $\beta_0 + \beta_1$ is the expected outcome when $D_i = 1$ — that's $\pi_A$. So $\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta$, and the OLS estimate $\hat\beta_1$ is numerically the same as $\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B$. ::: {#88ef3eb3 .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} from scipy.stats import t as t_dist # Stack the data Y = np.concatenate([A, B]) D = np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)]) # OLS by hand (or use scipy/statsmodels) import statsmodels.api as sm X_design = sm.add_constant(D) model = sm.OLS(Y, X_design).fit() beta0, beta1 = model.params se_b1 = model.bse[1] t_stat = model.tvalues[1] p_reg = model.pvalues[1] print(f"β̂₀ (≈ π_B) = {beta0:.4f} [true π_B = {pi_B}]") print(f"β̂₁ (≈ θ) = {beta1:.4f} [true θ = 0.04]") print(f"SE(β̂₁) = {se_b1:.5f}") print(f"t-statistic = {t_stat:.4f}") print(f"p-value = {p_reg:.4f}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` β̂₀ (≈ π_B) = 0.1990 [true π_B = 0.18] β̂₁ (≈ θ) = 0.0250 [true θ = 0.04] SE(β̂₁) = 0.01826 t-statistic = 1.3688 p-value = 0.1712 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#3f09b37a .cell execution_count=12} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ```{=html}
Two-sample z-test vs. OLS Regression: Side-by-Side Comparison
Two-sample z-test OLS regression
Point estimate 0.0250 0.0250
Standard error 0.01825 0.01826
Test statistic 1.3695 1.3688
p-value 0.1708 0.1712
``` ::: ::: The point estimates are identical — they must be, by algebra. The standard errors are *very* close but not exactly equal: OLS assumes a single pooled residual variance across both groups, while our two-sample formula lets each group have its own variance. For reasonably balanced experiments with similar conversion rates the difference is negligible. Why does this equivalence matter? Because the regression framework **scales**. Want to control for the day of the week a visitor arrived? Add a day-of-week dummy. Want to test three CTA variants simultaneously? Add another indicator. Want to check whether mobile users respond differently to the CTAs? Add an interaction term. All of that falls naturally out of regression, and the inference works exactly as it did here. ## The Problem with Peeking So far we've run our experiment, waited for all 1,000 visitors per group to arrive, and *then* looked at the result. But what if your boss is impatient? What if she says: *"Check the numbers after every 100 visitors — and if it's significant, we stop early and call a winner."* This practice is called **peeking**, and it is one of the most common mistakes in A/B testing. Here's why it's a problem. A single test at $\alpha = 0.05$ has a 5% chance of falsely rejecting $H_0$ when the null is actually true (a Type I error). But every time you peek, you get another roll of the dice. Each additional look is a new opportunity to get lucky and see a spuriously significant result. Across 10 peeks, the chance of at least one false positive is much higher than 5%. Let's quantify exactly how much higher. We simulate a world where $H_0$ is **true** ($\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20$, $\theta = 0$) and run 10,000 experiments. In each experiment, we peek at the results after 100, 200, …, 1,000 visitors per group. ::: {#6a2461bc .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} n_total = 1_000 peek_at = np.arange(100, n_total + 1, 100) # 10 peeks n_sims = 10_000 pi_null = 0.20 # H0: both groups equal false_positives = 0 for _ in range(n_sims): A_null = rng.binomial(1, pi_null, n_total) B_null = rng.binomial(1, pi_null, n_total) rejected = False for peek in peek_at: a_sub = A_null[:peek] b_sub = B_null[:peek] diff = a_sub.mean() - b_sub.mean() se_p = np.sqrt(a_sub.mean()*(1-a_sub.mean())/peek + b_sub.mean()*(1-b_sub.mean())/peek) if se_p > 0: z_p = diff / se_p p_p = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_p))) if p_p < 0.05: rejected = True break # stop the experiment early, as the boss wants if rejected: false_positives += 1 fpr = false_positives / n_sims print(f"Nominal false positive rate (single test): 5.0%") print(f"Empirical false positive rate (10 peeks): {fpr*100:.1f}%") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Nominal false positive rate (single test): 5.0% Empirical false positive rate (10 peeks): 19.4% ``` ::: ::: ::: {#09127a5a .cell execution_count=14} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Peeking dramatically inflates the false positive rate above the nominal 5% level.](index_files/figure-html/cell-15-output-1.png){width=567 height=374} ::: ::: The result is stark. A single properly-timed test keeps the false positive rate at its nominal 5%. But peeking 10 times — and stopping as soon as *any* peek is significant — inflates that rate to around **19%**. That means roughly one in four experiments will declare a winner that doesn't actually exist, simply because we kept looking. The practical lesson: **decide your sample size in advance, run the experiment to completion, and look at the results once.** If you genuinely need to peek (e.g., to catch a catastrophic failure), use methods designed for sequential testing — such as sequential probability ratio tests or alpha-spending functions — that maintain valid error control across multiple looks. Classical frequentist tests, run as described in this post, are calibrated for a single look only.
